lesson.menu_desc: Comparing value & spending smart abroad -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 10 -- Shopping & Smart Spending
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 10 -- Shopping & Smart Spending

reading.rtitle: The Souvenir Trap: How Travellers Spend Smart Abroad
paras: Every famous destination has its shopping streets, and most of them are designed to part you from your money. The closer you stand to the main attraction, the more overpriced everything becomes. A fridge magnet that costs one euro three streets away can be five euros in the square, and the so-called 'local craft' is, more often than not, a knock-off shipped in by the thousand.

The traveller who spends smart treats the first price as the beginning of a conversation, not the end. In many markets, the marked price is considerably higher than what the seller actually expects to receive, and a polite shopper who is willing to haggle can often talk the price down by a third. The trick is to stay friendly, to browse without rushing, and never to look too keen on any single item.

Yet smart spending is not only about paying less. It is about value for money. Sometimes the cheapest option is a false economy, and the slightly pricier, better-made piece is far more worth the extra. The wisest souvenirs are rarely the most expensive ones; they are the ones that still mean something long after the trip, when the bargain has been forgotten but the memory has not.
reading.source: Adapted from a travel-money column, The Frugal Globe

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main message?
choices: Travellers should always buy the cheapest souvenir available.
Spending smart means avoiding tourist traps, being ready to haggle, and choosing value over the lowest price.
The best souvenirs are the most expensive 'local crafts' in the main square.

items: Prices tend to rise the closer you get to the main attraction.
The closer you stand to the attraction, the more overpriced everything becomes.
The writer says the marked price in a market is usually exactly what you must pay.
It is often considerably higher than what the seller actually expects; you can haggle.
A polite shopper willing to haggle can sometimes cut the price by about a third.
The text says they can talk the price down by a third.
According to the writer, the cheapest option is always the best value.
The cheapest can be a false economy; value for money matters more than price.

matching.title: Match each expression to its meaning
defs: a cheap fake copy of a brand-name product
something far too expensive for what it is; a bad deal
how much quality you get for the price you pay
a saving now that costs you more in the long run
costing more than it is really worth
to argue politely to bring a price down

lf.title: Intensify your comparisons
items: expensive than the one round the corner.
better made than the cheap copies.
good a bargain as it first looks.
A seller asks 60 for a scarf you know is worth about 35. React, then try to talk the price down.
You see the 'same' bag far cheaper at another stall. Compare the two using far more / slightly / considerably.
A friend wants to buy a cheap phone charger that looks like a knock-off. Warn them it could be a false economy.
You are deciding between a pricey, well-made jacket and a cheap one. Say which is better value for money, and why.

rephrase.title: Rewrite each sentence with the intensifier in brackets
items: The market is cheaper than the mall.
This copy is a little worse than the original.
Haggling here saves a lot of money.
The square is more expensive than the side streets.
The bargain version is not as good as the real one.

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: The market is far cheaper than the mall.
This copy is slightly worse than the original.
The more you haggle, the more money you save.
The square is considerably more expensive than the side streets.
The bargain version is not nearly as good as the real one.
answerkey.note: Form check: far / considerably / slightly + comparative; not nearly as + adjective + as; the more..., the more....

listenings.text: Welcome to the old market, my friend. Let me give you one honest tip before you start. Up here, by the main gate, everything is considerably more expensive, and a lot of it is overpriced. Those 'handmade' bowls? Most are knock-offs, far cheaper just two streets down. The marked prices are only a starting point, so do not be shy to haggle. A friendly shopper who takes their time can usually talk me down by a third, sometimes more. And remember: the cheapest stall is not always the best. A slightly pricier piece that is properly made is often far better value for money than a bargain that breaks on the plane home.

I used to be a terrible shopper when I travelled. I would pay the first price out of politeness, and I came home with a suitcase full of overpriced rubbish. These days I am far more careful. I browse slowly, I never look too keen, and I am always ready to haggle. The more relaxed I am, the more the price seems to drop. But the biggest change is how I think about money now. I no longer chase the cheapest thing, because the cheapest is often a false economy. I look for value for money, for the piece that is considerably better made and will still mean something to me years later.

extra_audio.text: First, on a tight budget, she decided to shop around before buying anything. Then she browsed the stalls, which were far cheaper than the tourist shops. She spotted a knock-off bag that was badly overpriced and walked away. At the next stall she haggled and talked the seller down by ten euros. Finally, she bought a jacket that was such good value for money it felt like a steal.
